summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 13 lineup, finding Apple focused exactly where it should: cameras and battery life. All four models are slightly thicker and heavier than their iPhone 12 counterparts, a tradeoff he considers worthwhile. The 13 Pro's camera module now matches the Pro Max's, and Apple has shifted from hiding the camera bump to proudly emphasizing it. The A15 chip delivers modest speed gains but dramatic efficiency improvements, and ProMotion's adaptive refresh rates paradoxically extend battery life rather than draining it. He argues the Pro iPhones are now better understood as cameras with phones rather than phones with cameras.
key_points: Apple's two most impactful improvements — camera and battery life — came at the cost of slightly thicker, heavier devices, a tradeoff Gruber considers obvious and correct
The camera bump has evolved from an 'embarrassing bulge' Apple tried to hide in 2014 to a design element the company now proudly showcases in marketing
ProMotion's adaptive refresh rates actually improve battery life for common tasks like video streaming, unlike Android's less sophisticated high refresh rate implementations
The 13 Pro Max more than doubled its streaming video battery life year-over-year, with the 13 Pro jumping from 11 to 20 hours
Cinematic mode is fun but limited to 1080p/30fps, and curiously lacks a 24fps option despite Apple calling 24fps 'film style' elsewhere in Settings
money_quotes: The two ways that Apple could best improve the day-to-day utility of using an iPhone are improving the camera system and improving battery life. And those are exactly where Apple focused its attention for this year's new iPhones.
I've been arguing for years that iPhones are evolving such that they're better thought of as cameras than 'phones'. Now, there's no need to argue about it.
I think it's fair to say that the iPhone 13 and 13 Mini are phones with very good cameras; the 13 Pro and Pro Max are excellent cameras with phones.
Computational photography is upending the entire camera world. It's hard to beat the physics of good glass and large sensors, but math is doing pretty good — and gaining fast.
The Pro Max more than doubled its battery life for streaming video playback in a year.

The adaptive nature of ProMotion means that not only does it not hurt battery life, like the less sophisticated high refresh rate features on Android phones, but it actually helps extend battery life for common tasks like watching video or reading text.
Yes, the iPhone is late to the high refresh rate game, but as is often the case with Apple, it was worth waiting for.
key_insight: The iPhone 13 Pro lineup represents a turning point where iPhones are definitively cameras first and phones second, with Apple's patience on ProMotion paying off through adaptive refresh rates that improve both visual quality and battery life simultaneously.
